macro_segments.title: Introduction and Review of Shevet (Lost Wages) Calculation

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and background on shevet compensation
Examples of shevet assessment by injury type (hand, leg, blindness)

macro_segments.title: Rava's Question on Multiple Injuries: Assessment Timing and Tzaar/Boshet Compounding

micro_segments.title: Scenario of successive injuries without intermediate assessment

macro_segments.title: Introduction and Review of Shevet (Lost Wages) Calculation (continued)

micro_segments.title: Tzaar and boshet as distinct from nezek and shevet

macro_segments.title: Rava's Question on Multiple Injuries: Assessment Timing and Tzaar/Boshet Compounding (continued)

micro_segments.title: Whether to assess all injuries at once or separately for tzaar and boshet
Teiku: unresolved question left by Rava
Discussion of Rosh's position versus Tosafot Yom Tov on deciding teiku

macro_segments.title: Rava's Question on Shevet for Temporary vs. Permanent Injuries

micro_segments.title: Question: does shevet apply to injuries that heal completely?
Case of bruise on hand that will heal versus permanent damage
Proof from braita about hitting parents: shevet for temporary injury
Case of depilatory cream on bald jester: assessing shevet for loss of job

macro_segments.title: Abaye and Rava's Dispute: Shevet Gedola versus Shevet Ketana for Healing Injuries

micro_segments.title: Abaye's position: two types of shevet (large and small) for healing injuries
Rava's position: only temporary daily shevet, no permanent valuation
Application to eved Ivri (Hebrew servant) whose hand is cut off
Abaye vs. Rava on eved Ivri: who receives large and small shevet payments
Mishnah principle: boshet assessed by both insulter and insulted
Identifying the tanna: cannot be Rabbi Meir or Rabbi Yehuda, must be Rabbi Shimon
Rabbi Meir's position: all Jews equal standard (bnei Avraham, Yitzchak, Yaakov)
Rabbi Yehuda's position: rich and poor assessed differently
Rabbi Shimon's position: wealthy bumped down, poor to lowest level

macro_segments.title: Requirement of Intention (Kavanah) for Boshet Liability

micro_segments.title: Sleeping person causing embarrassment: exempt without intention
Braita on wrong target: embarrassing child instead of adult
Teaching that boshet requires intention, compared to murder vs. manslaughter
Source from case of woman pulling attacker's clothes in struggle

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Special Cases: Naked, Blind, Sleeping, and Falling from Roof

micro_segments.title: Embarrassing someone naked, blind, or asleep creates liability
Sleeping person who embarrasses others is exempt without intention
Falling from roof: pay nezek but exempt from boshet without intention

macro_segments.title: Comparative Embarrassment: Naked vs. Clothed; Bathhouse vs. Marketplace; Intent to Act

micro_segments.title: Embarrassing naked person is less serious than embarrassing clothed person
Embarrassing in bathhouse less serious than in marketplace
Rav Pappa on wind blowing clothes: partially revealing is less embarrassing than pulling clothes off
Bathhouse embarrassment means semi-public bathing by river, not private bathhouse

macro_segments.title: Embarassing Asleep Person Then Dying: Kisufa vs. Ziluta Framework

micro_segments.title: Question: is boshet paid if person embarrassed while asleep then dies?
Two frameworks: kisufa (personal emotional hurt) vs. ziluta (societal degradation)
Dead person cannot experience kisufa, but societal standing still affected

macro_segments.title: Braita on Boshet for Deaf, Child, Fool: Who Has Rights to Embarrassment

micro_segments.title: Deaf and child have boshet rights; fool (shoteh) does not
Testing ziluta interpretation: even fool would have family embarrassment
Counter-argument: fool already maximally embarrassing to family
Rav Pappa: kisufa (personal awareness) applies even to children based on age

macro_segments.title: Determining Boshet Basis: Family Embarrassment vs. Personal Emotional Harm

micro_segments.title: Rav Pappa's unresolved question: kisufa (personal) vs. boshet mishpacha (family)
Testing family thesis against child embarrassment
Testing family thesis against fool: fool already maximum family embarrassment
Rav Pappa reconsidered: kisufa can apply to children at appropriate ages

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbi Yehuda vs. Rabbi Shimon (tanna dispute on boshet)
